sentences.text: Hey Shannon, can you hear me all right?
Yeah, sorry about that.
Not at all, not at all.
Busy afternoon?
Yes.
We're glad you found some time.
Um, I do have a hard stop at the top of the hour, but we can definitely cover most of the tool through, uh, the next 20 minutes.
I'd love to start just knowing a little bit about what your exposure to apartment IQ has been so far.
Um, I know that it's a thing.

I haven't actually been in it, so I have, I know I have some teams that are using it, um, and I know that it, my understanding is it kind of scrapes data from um websites basically to provide us with.
Uh, like a quick, easy market survey that we could, you know, get if we looked on websites, but this does it for us.
So I think it's a time saver and, you know, kind of the way that our folks would be doing it.
I would assume it's probably more accurate than if our people are trying to pop it into a spreadsheet too, but I'd love to see like what reporting's available, um.
And then, yeah, if I, if it's possible for me to get access to the properties in my portfolio, that would be awesome too.
Definitely, yeah, we'll absolutely set you up.
I just need to know what those properties are, and I'll get my team to have you added uh to those specific market surveys so you don't have a bunch of clutter.
uh, and I'll walk you through a couple of the key workflows here.
So, um, the screen we're looking at now, this is the landing page when you do log into apartment IQ.
All the market surveys you have access to will be listed here.
Uh, go ahead and just throw ourselves into one of them.
Let's go over here to Dallas.
When you open up a market survey, uh, there are several tabs at the top that you would use to navigate through the entirety of the CompStat reports.
The first is this digitized market survey.
We start reading this on the left, you have your different metrics that apartment IQ collates for us.
These are all calculated and defined right here as you mouse your cursor over them.
Things like NER exposure percentages.
Everything that we're able to pull from websites and then funnel into these available vacant, applied, and leased buckets.
Mhm.
And in the entirety of your comp set for this particular market.
Comps are completely editable, they're never set in stone.
It's really easy to make changes, for instance, top right hand corner.
You have an interactive map on the right.
Your subject property highlighted in this purple.
The additional properties in your concept highlighted and numbered.
And then all these black dots represent other properties that you could potentially add to your concept if you wanted.
Will it take a concept for you when you first Set it up.
Absolutely right, yeah, there's two ways that most of my partners do it.

Uh, recently, a lot of the Cushman properties that come in, we are generating comps for, but you can also define an existing comp set and then ask us to add any additional best fit comps to round out maybe a 10 or 12 property comp set.
OK.
We define comps with a comp score that's just based on distance to your subject property, a differential of pricing and square footage by the unit to make sure that it's a really relevant comp.
Uh, and then of course, things like amenities, uh, and your built are also factored in.
So it's a complex algorithm looking at a couple of different metrics there to develop your compet and develop those rankings.
Once they're added in here, you can click through and learn anything about these properties by clicking on a property title, for instance.
I can see every unit that they're showing available on their website.
I can see the unit's pricing history if I wanted to.
The floor plans pricing history?
The unit style, whether it's a classic or picking it up as a renovated unit.
Advertised status, is it available?
Is it leased, uh, or is it an applied status?
We can also see things like how often the rent's changing here, number of price changes, total price to date, how long it's been on the market.
We can also sort these lists.
Number of price changes in what time frame?
The entirety of the time that it's been offered online and listing available.
We'll have leased units as well.
So everything that's leased in the last 30 days.
How does it know?
Oh, we scrape websites every single day.
So every single day we're keeping a track record of what's available.
When something gets removed from the website, we put that unit in an applied status for 7 days.
By the 8th day, if that unit remains off the website, it starts to get counted as a lease for the last 7.
As the weeks go by, it'll stay in the leases for the last 30, and that's how we count.
OK, so if it pops back up, then you will put throw it back in inventory.
As a relised unit, for instance, yeah, right, absolutely right, and it'll stay here as a as as on market.
Finally, we have the all units list, which is our reconstructed rent roll.
So apartment IQ, not just a pricing and availability tool, we also have 3 now approaching 4 years of historical information on pricing and availability.
So we can surface trends.
We'll talk about those in a little bit, but because of those 34 years we've been tracking these properties, we've probably seen every unit at least once.
And so that's how we're able to deliver for you a reconstructed rent roll, tell you what the last lease price was, what the best terms were, uh, and everything about that property.
We also have the ability to turn this market survey from point in time to a summary of the last 7 days, the last 30, and so on.
All the way 3 years back.
Yeah, depending on what's relevant for your analysis and then the exporting of this tool we talked about what kind of reporting we have.
The first tool is a PDF export.
We often refer to this as kind of the market summary because it's going to generate a high level report for you.
We also have the Excel report here, which is a much more detailed and granular report.
I definitely recommend you take a look at it.
What I like about the Excel in particular is that it's going to honor and respect any configuration that you have here in your digital market survey.
So for instance, if we've customized these metrics a little bit.
Perhaps we've pulled out a particular unit style.
We don't care about the classics for today's analysis.

We only want to see renovated, for instance, or maybe we're highlighting a couple of subproperties within this market to create a really core submarket analysis that Excel export will respect all this configuration here.
You'll have your key pricing and availability information.
Your fees and deposits that we can pull from websites.
Mhm.
And then amenities both in unit and at the community level.
All this information will be available for you in that Excel export.
The PDF export which pops right up here conveniently in your web browser.
It'll be branded for Cushman and Wakefield.
It'll have that stock photo from the website.
It'll even be dated so that you know you're looking at the most up to-date data.
Apartment IQ, it runs every evening.
So about 7:30 Eastern, we go ahead and we take a look at all the updates and we push those for you the following morning.
So you're always looking at last night's information.
It starts with our high level market summary, where are we geographically and 4 key benchmarking indicators.
And then we dive right into property level insights complete with trend information as well over the last 7 days.
So if this was the only tool you had, you knew a little bit more, you know a little bit more about where this number's going and how it's changed.
You'll notice here, and I'll just uh zoom in just a little bit there for concession values that have ended, we do note the timing of those concessions.
Any new concessions will have a green new next to them rather than the end.
Uh, so that means The, sorry
about that
it is like they, they started offering a bigger concession.
Or
yeah,
right, it has either shrunk or grown based on the percentage change in the value of the average concession.
In fact, here's a really good one where you can see the icon at Ross just started advertising a new concession, and that was valued at $667 on average.
OK.

We summarize it through the bed types and then finally for our marketing folks, we have one concise page that lists all the concessions in the market as well as what units they may apply to and any other details that go with it.
So this is a really popular report for most folks when they get theirselves set up with apartment IQ.
Oftentimes this will be the first report that they're working with most clearly.
I will

open up the I'll actually send you an email with the Excel report just because opening and sharing Excel is a little annoying on my computer, but I'll send you what that looks like just so you have an idea of how it's broken down.
OK.
And that is our market survey.
We continue to leverage our historic information in this floor plans report, which is a bit more of a detailed and nuanced view of what's going on by the bed type.
So you'll find, for instance, here in this particular market, we start with studio apartment floor plans.
We have floor plan names, there's square footage, this list being ordered by the square footage, and then a unit mix information, what's leased and what the lease rents were.
As well as the availability that's remaining.
And the rents among what's available.
As you scroll through and I'll link my window just a little bit bigger, as you scroll through all of your subject property units will be highlighted in this indigo.

And then you can really see how the trends are working within a particular floor plan to kind of see if there's maybe increased appetite for a larger one by one, for instance, or a smaller one by one if there's a particular square footage that is really moving quite nicely or a square footage value that is not moving as well.
And this can be ordered by the floor plan or by the bed type.
Continuing on the historical point, these 3 reports are probably where most people spend the majority of their time.
You'll find here our historical page that illustrates all the historic trends that we're kind of summarizing in our market surveys.
So here we're gonna continue to look at the last 12 months.
We'll start with a retro analysis of the minimum rents in your market.
The indigo line again representing the subject property in this case, that green representing the comp range and then the green line being the median.
So we can see how much or how how close we are to that median, what the spread is.
We can view this at the market level and at the property level.
So if I wanted to clear this entirely because it's a little busy and compare just my property to perhaps a comp next door.
I can do so.
I can also see how these minimum rent trends have interacted with newly listed units over the same period.

This has been a really great tool for folks to say, hey, I'd love to, you know, raise our rents and capitalize on the fact that our exposure is low, but we recently had a comp that just threw 50 units on the market, and I need to adjust for that.
Or this is why the pricing went down earlier last year because you can see what's been happening with new listed units, for instance.
And that can be contracted
that account or yeah, that's a volume of new units they put on in that week.
Correct.
By the week at this point, how many new units were listed available on their website?
You can change the interval as well.
So we're looking at weekly, uh, monthly, quarterly and yearly, I guess if you're looking at the uh at the last several years.
But I find weekly and monthly to be the most helpful way to look at this tool.
Yeah.
We'll have net leased units too, so anything that's come off a website, not just as applied, because remember we do distinguish between an applied unit and a leased unit.
So these are units that have stayed off websites and are very confidently considered a lease.
And just to track velocity a little bit better, we have the net leased units by month broken out here.
Finally, exposure and the inverse, of course, we have at least 2, least percentage, by the market or by the property.
Very cool.
Yeah, so this is where most people spend their time.
There is a subgroup within the uh CushmanWakefield community that is using our explore tool in conjunction with a research report.
I showed you the market survey first because a research report is just a reconstructed market survey for an unknowned asset.
Which you might discover using our explore tool which allows us to choose any zip code.
Uh, do you have one offhand perhaps, Shannon, one you work closely with?
Let's go to DeKalb County, Georgia.
So perhaps I'm here in Georgia.
I'm looking for a very specific kind of property that I want to get involved with, perhaps I'm pitching new business or just looking to learn about a new market.
I'll use these filters here at the top.
Oh, maybe that was a bad choice for this market because it seems like there's only 1 or 2.
Let's go with garden style.
Right, a little bit more.
So maybe I'm in the BTR space, the SFR space.
I'm looking at these smaller properties.
I'm gonna set a total unit value, no more.
Let's do a maximum of 100, for instance, uh, to really hone in on some of these BTR properties.
I can even throw in a year built or specific amenities that makes sense, like, uh, perhaps detached garage or, or a private backyard.
What you'll notice here on the left is that 30 day market snapshot so we can understand exactly what's available here within properties that fit our parameters.
And then what the historicals have been like.
Once I'm in here and I see a property that looks interesting to me, I can go ahead and rip this new report button.

I'm not going to do it here because I don't want to muddy up the Cushman existing reports, but when I create a new report, Apartment IQ is going to take this property, use it as the subject property, and then run our comp algorithm against this property to create.
One of these research reports here for you.
OK.
And it'll be the same exact functionality as your market survey.
So if you're familiar with the market survey, you're familiar with the research report, the only thing that changes is that it's an unknowned asset.
So the color scheme is just a little bit different where you saw Indigo, you'll now see this orange, but it's otherwise the same.
You can still click in and drill down to see exactly what units are being counted where.
The same floor plans and historical reports as well, and the same exports.
So this has been a great tool for folks who are pitching new business or looking to uh get started with the pro forma, for instance, um.
That's kind of the two camps here, uh, right now.
Makes sense so far?
Yeah.
What do we think?
I love it.
I don't know why everybody wouldn't have it.
I think the same thing.
It's a no-brainer.
Um, I'm gonna go ahead and get you added here.
It just looks manage comps manage users.
Let me just make sure you're not already in.
OK, look, we do have an account for you.
You do?
How did I get that?
Probably really early on, you only have access to 17 properties.
Collins off Coulter, Copeland.
How big is your portfolio?
I just wanna make sure we can accommodate everything for you.
It's like 200 and some odd properties.
OK, so what is happening, I'll tell you, not all properties are opting in immediately.
We're kind of going piecemeal and working through different portfolios at a time where we get access to managing directors and, and ownership groups, um, so that's kind of how we're slowly building.
I can get you in touch with our sales director who's been spearheading kind of the contracting of these new.
Uh, portfolio groups and he can share with you a list of what's already in here so you can compare it against what you have in your portfolio.
I'm gonna change you to a manager.
The distinction there is are these roles here we have the read only person who is typically that ownership group, maybe that third party or or someone not in the customer organization.
They can get access to apartment IQ, but they can't change anything.
An editor can view and manage the comps just for their assigned markets.
The manager can do the same plus invite users.
So anyone on your team, you'll be able to invite them into apartment IQ and give them access to the same things you have access to.
So we set you up as that manager.
And you'll, when you log in, I'll send you the login link shortly.
I just have another 30 minute call, Shannon, and I'll be able to get this information to you.
Um, and you'll be able to hit the ground running tomorrow.
And as you're using the tool, do not hesitate to reach out to me.
If you have any additional questions, I'm happy to jump on another one of these calls and talk through any specifics you have as you poked around a little bit.
Awesome.
Sound like a plan?
Yeah, thank you.
You're very welcome.
I hope you have a great rest of your week.
I know it's already Thursday.
Um, happy holidays.
I, I am out of the office after Monday of next week, uh, through the new year, so I will talk to you, uh, later than that.
Um, so enjoy the holiday break and we'll talk soon, OK?
Awesome, thank you so much.
You're very welcome, Shannon.
Take care now.
You too.
Bye.
Bye-bye.
